title: Her Last Letter
content:
آ آ آ آ June 4th! Do you know what that date means?
آ آ آ آ June 4th! By this air and these pines!
آ آ آ آ Well, only you know how I hate scenes,
آ آ آ آ These might be my very last lines!
آ آ آ آ For perhaps, sir, youï؟½ll kindly remember
آ آ آ آ If some other things youï؟½ve forgot
آ آ آ آ That you last wrote the 4th of december,
آ آ آ آ Just six months ago! from this spot;

آ آ آ آ From this spot, that you said was ï؟½the fairest
آ آ آ آ For once being held in my thought.ï؟½
آ آ آ آ Now, really I call that the barest
آ آ آ آ Of well, I wonï؟½t say what I ought!
آ آ آ آ For here I am back from my ï؟½riches,ï؟½
آ آ آ آ My ï؟½triumphs,ï؟½ my ï؟½tours,ï؟½ and all that;
آ آ آ آ And youï؟½re not to be found in the ditches
آ آ آ آ Or temples of Poverty Flat!

آ آ آ آ From Paris we went for the season
آ آ آ آ To London, when Pa wired, ï؟½Stop.ï؟½
آ آ آ آ Mama says ï؟½his healthï؟½ was the reason.
آ آ آ آ (Iï؟½ve heard that some things took a ï؟½drop.ï؟½)
آ آ آ آ But she said if my patience Iï؟½d summon
آ آ آ آ I could go back with him to the Flat
آ آ آ آ Perhaps I was thinking of some one
آ آ آ آ Who of me well was not thinking that!

آ آ آ آ Of course you will say that I ï؟½never
آ آ آ آ Replied to the letter you wrote.ï؟½
آ آ آ آ That is just like a man! But, however,
آ آ آ آ I read it or how could I quote?
آ آ آ آ And as to the stories youï؟½ve heard (No,
آ آ آ آ Donï؟½t tell me you havenï؟½t I know!),
آ آ آ آ Youï؟½ll not believe one blessed word, Joe;
آ آ آ آ But just whence they came, let them go!

آ آ آ آ And they came from Sade Lotski of Yolo,
آ آ آ آ Whose father sold clothes on the Bar
آ آ آ آ You called him Job-lotski, you know, Joe,
آ آ آ آ And the boys said her value was par.
آ آ آ آ Well, we met her in Paris just flaring
آ آ آ آ With diamonds, and lost in a hat
آ آ آ آ And she asked me ï؟½how Joseph was faring
آ آ آ آ In his love-suit on Poverty Flat!ï؟½

آ آ آ آ She thought it would shame me! I met her
آ آ آ آ With a look, Joe, that made her eyes drop;
آ آ آ آ And I said that your ï؟½love-suit fared better
آ آ آ آ Than any suit out of their shop!ï؟½
آ آ آ آ And I didnï؟½t blush then as Iï؟½m doing
آ آ آ آ To find myself here, all alone,
آ آ آ آ And left, Joe, to do all the ï؟½sueingï؟½
آ آ آ آ To a lover thatï؟½s certainly flown.

آ آ آ آ In this brand-new hotel, called ï؟½The Lilyï؟½
آ آ آ آ (I wonder who gave it that name?)
آ آ آ آ I really am feeling quite silly,
آ آ آ آ To think I was once called the same;
آ آ آ آ And I stare from its windows, and fancy
آ آ آ آ Iï؟½m labeled to each passer-by.
آ آ آ آ Ah! gone is the old necromancy,
آ آ آ آ For nothing seems right to my eye.

آ آ آ آ On that hill there are stores that I knew not;
آ آ آ آ Thereï؟½s a street where I once lost my way;
آ آ آ آ And the copse where you once tied my shoe-knot
آ آ آ آ Is shamelessly open as day!
آ آ آ آ And that bank by the spring I once drank there,
آ آ آ آ And you called the place Eden, you know;
آ آ آ آ Now Iï؟½m banished like Eve though the bank there
آ آ آ آ Is belonging to ï؟½Adams and Co.ï؟½

آ آ آ آ Thereï؟½s the rustle of silk on the sidewalk;
آ آ آ آ Just now there passed by a tall hat;
آ آ آ آ But thereï؟½s gloom in this ï؟½boomï؟½ and this wild talk
آ آ آ آ Of the ï؟½futureï؟½ of Poverty Flat.
آ آ آ آ Thereï؟½s a decorous chill in the air, Joe,
آ آ آ آ Where once we were simple and free;
آ آ آ آ And I hear theyï؟½ve been making a mayor, Joe,
آ آ آ آ Of the man who shot Sandy McGee.

آ آ آ آ But thereï؟½s still the ï؟½lap, lapï؟½ of the river;
آ آ آ آ Thereï؟½s the song of the pines, deep and low.
آ آ آ آ (How my longing for them made me quiver
آ آ آ آ In the park that they call Fontainebleau!)
آ آ آ آ Thereï؟½s the snow-peak that looked on our dances,
آ آ آ آ And blushed when the morning said, ï؟½Go!ï؟½
آ آ آ آ Thereï؟½s a lot that remains which one fancies
آ آ آ آ But somehow thereï؟½s never a Joe!

آ آ آ آ Perhaps, on the whole, it is better,
آ آ آ آ For you might have been changed like the rest;
آ آ آ آ Though itï؟½s strange that Iï؟½m trusting this letter
آ آ آ آ To papa, just to have it addressed.
آ آ آ آ He thinks he may find you, and really
آ آ آ آ Seems kinder now Iï؟½m all alone.
آ آ آ آ You might have been here, Joe, if merely
آ آ آ آ To look what Iï؟½m willing to own.

آ آ آ آ Well, well! thatï؟½s all past; so good-night, Joe;
آ آ آ آ Good-night to the river and Flat;
آ آ آ آ Good-night to whatï؟½s wrong and whatï؟½s right, Joe;
آ آ آ آ Good-night to the past, and all that
آ آ آ آ To Harrisonï؟½s barn, and its dancers;
آ آ آ آ To the moon, and the white peak of snow;
آ آ آ آ And good-night to the canyon that answers
آ آ آ آ My ï؟½Joe!ï؟½ with its echo of ï؟½No!ï؟½

آ آ آ آ P.S.

آ آ آ آ Iï؟½ve just got your note. You deceiver!
آ آ آ آ How dared you how could you? Oh, Joe!
آ آ آ آ To think Iï؟½ve been kept a believer
آ آ آ آ In things that were six months ago!
آ آ آ آ And itï؟½s youï؟½ve built this house, and the bank, too,
آ آ آ آ And the mills, and the stores, and all that!
آ آ آ آ And for everything changed I must thank you,
آ آ آ آ Who have ï؟½struck itï؟½ on Poverty Flat!

آ آ آ آ How dared you get rich you great stupid!
آ آ آ آ Like papa, and some men that I know,
آ آ آ آ Instead of just trusting to Cupid
آ آ آ آ And to me for your money? Ah, Joe!
آ آ آ آ Just to think you sent never a word, dear,
آ آ آ آ Till you wrote to papa for consent!
آ آ آ آ Now I know why they had me transferred here,
آ آ آ آ And ï؟½the health of papaï؟½ what that meant!

آ آ آ آ Now I know why they call this ï؟½The Lily;ï؟½
آ آ آ آ Why the man who shot Sandy McGee
آ آ آ آ You made mayor! ï؟½Twas because oh, you silly!
آ آ آ آ He once ï؟½went down the middleï؟½ with me!
آ آ آ آ Iï؟½ve been fooled to the top of my bent here,
آ آ آ آ So come, and ask pardon you know
آ آ آ آ That youï؟½ve still got to get my consent, dear!
آ آ آ آ And just think what that echo said Joe!
meaning:
This evocative piece by Bret Harte (Francis) represents a masterful exploration of classic. The lines capture a profound emotional resonance, inviting the reader to reflect on the deeper themes of human experience and artistic expression.

In the broader context of Bret Harte (Francis)'s bibliography, these specific lines stand out for their clarity and poignancy. Whether you are searching for inspiration or a moment of quiet contemplation, this work offers a timeless perspective that transcends its original era.

At Linespedia, we have curated this specific poem to ensure it reaches a modern audience. We recommend sharing these lines as a digital poster or using them as a thoughtful caption to connect with others who appreciate the enduring beauty of verse.
